page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.r7a.2xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual server instance powered by the KVM hypervisor. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 8 physical cores of an AMD EPYC 7T83 processor running at a base frequency of 2.55 GHz. The system is equipped with 64.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and relies on a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Benchmark results indicate strong single-core CPU performance, with stress-ng single-core tests placing in the top 25%. Multi-core CPU performance, software compilation, and cryptography operations fall within the average tier. However, memory performance shows distinct tradeoffs; while cached small-block read bandwidth is average at 516.15 GB/sec, uncached large-block read and write bandwidths are weak, scoring 37.69 GB/sec and 17.66 GB/sec respectively. This pattern is mirrored in the weak PassMark memory and database operations scores.

Given its high memory density and dedicated CPU allocation, this instance provides a stable environment for memory-intensive workloads that do not require high-speed local storage. It is well-suited for in-memory caching systems like Redis, which achieved 7.68 million operations per second in testing, as well as medium-scale web serving and light text or image processing. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the instance offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio on dedicated AMD EPYC hardware, making it a viable option for memory-bound applications, though workloads requiring high sequential memory throughput or heavy database transactions may encounter performance bottlenecks.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.r7a.2xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual server instance featuring 8 dedicated vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 7T83 processor running at 2.55 GHz. It is configured with 64.0 GB of RAM, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB, but lacks local storage and GPU accelerators. Performance benchmarks show strong single-core CPU execution, though multi-core operations and software compilation remain in the average tier. Uncached memory bandwidth for large blocks is relatively weak, which impacts sequential memory-intensive tasks and database operations. With a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps, this instance is best suited for memory-intensive workloads such as in-memory caching, static web serving, and lightweight text or image processing where dedicated CPU resources and high memory capacity are required without the need for high-speed local storage.
